debates.title: Is reality rendered — and by what?
debates.crux: Three books reject self-sufficient matter and reach nearly the same conclusion through incompatible mechanisms. The agreement is genuine; so is the unresolved disagreement about what does the rendering.

positions.stance: A frequency domain interpreted by a holographic brain — reality as a projection unfolded from Bohm's implicate order.
A computed, information-based world whose quantum and cosmological quirks read like the render budget of a simulation.
A rule-governed virtual experience generated by consciousness itself, which is the computer running it.

debates.title: Produced, fundamental, or received?
debates.crux: Is consciousness generated by the brain, fundamental to reality, or received from elsewhere? The books split three ways, and several deliberately refuse to close the question.

positions.stance: Fundamental and participatory — a distributed, holographic process inside an undivided whole.
The primary substrate: matter and brains are virtual constructs within consciousness.
Science's black box — surveyed as brain-generated, DNA-encoded, or radio-received, landing on a DNA inference the authors flag as their own.
Possibly a player outside the render operating a body inside it — kept open as a bet, not a result.

debates.title: What kind of event is a contact report?
debates.crux: When someone reports contact, abduction, or impossible perception, is it literal, psychological, cultural, or something the current categories can't hold? The books read the same reports very differently.

positions.stance: Real-and-imaginary at once — bracket the ultimate cause and study how the experience becomes socially real.
Treat the experience as a datum — survey abduction accounts while holding the interpretation open.
A transpersonal event — the psyche reaching a deeper order the ordinary senses don't reach.
A valid data stream from the larger consciousness system, read through its own model.

debates.title: What counts when the subject resists verification?
debates.crux: With a subject that dodges ordinary proof, which evidence should weigh most — first-person experience, documents and testimony, participant observation, or patterns in anomalies?

positions.stance: First-person experiment: run the protocol yourself; third-person proof was never the point.
Documents, testimony, and insider disclosure, read the way an intelligence analyst reads sources.
Participant-observation ethnography — study the believers and the effects, not only the claim.
Catalogs of anomalies a materialist model cannot absorb, offered as pressure on the model.

debates.title: Two roads out of the ordinary self
debates.crux: Two practice books dissolve the everyday self to reach a fuller presence — but by different roads and on different metaphysics.

positions.stance: Step out of compulsive thought into the Now; the ego is a pain-body sustained by identification.
Recover Quality — the pre-intellectual event before subject and object divide — through care and attention.

bridges.title: Encounter states ↔ the holographic mind
bridges.claim: Transpersonal and 'encounter' states — Grof's psychedelic sessions, near-death and out-of-body reports, dreamtime material — resemble a mind reconstructing a whole world from a frequency domain rather than receiving it through the senses.
bridges.disclosure: An interpretive resemblance, not evidence that either explains the other. Talbot offers these as extensions of the holographic model that Bohm and Pribram did not all endorse; reading a contact report as a holographic event is a reader's hypothesis, not a demonstrated mechanism.

bridges.title: A rendered world ↔ suffering as curriculum
bridges.claim: If physical life is a training simulation that evolves consciousness (Campbell) or a rendered world we log into (Virk), then the practice books' work on ego, pain, and acceptance can be read as the 'learning objectives' of that environment.
bridges.disclosure: A cross-section analogy the reader draws, not a claim either side makes. Campbell frames life as learning; Tolle and Pirsig describe inner work on its own terms. Neither endorses the other, and the usefulness of the practice does not confirm the simulation.

bridges.title: Managing belief ↔ what counts as evidence
bridges.claim: The same forces that shape what feels real — Vallee's 'strategic denial', institutional framing, media and manipulation — also decide which anomalous evidence is ever taken seriously. How belief is managed sits upstream of how evidence is weighed.
bridges.disclosure: This links a mechanism (belief can be steered) to a consequence (evidence gets filtered). It asserts no specific cover-up; only that belief-shaping and evidence-reception are entangled and worth reading together.
